 "title": "Five-to-seven co-equal incumbents plus an import price floor turn every entrant win into a contested knife-fight, pinning Year-1 pace to the band floor",
 "narrative": "shape.cr3's 0.55 is extrapolated from a single 15.8% Schneider datapoint; the actual evidence shows five to seven co-equal 'leading' vendors (Schneider, Eaton, Vertiv, ABB, Legrand, Siemens, nVent) and only qualitative 'medium/semi-consolidated' labels, with 60–70% import dependence in Eastern Europe setting a low-cost price floor. That structure is worse for an entrant than a tight oligopoly: more scaled players able and motivated to price-match any pilot win inside their accounts, bundling PDUs with UPS and busway lines the entrant doesn't carry. The model prices incumbent response at exactly zero — no source quantifies it, and an unquantified competitive response is a risk, not a comfort. The reference-class outcome is win-rates and cycle times that pin Year-1 obtainable share at the 0.01 band floor rather than the 0.03 mid.",

The data center PDU market is estimated to be valued at USD 4.89 billion in 2026 and USD 14.23 billion by 2032, registering a CAGR of 19.5% from 2026 to 2032. The increasing expansion of hyperscale and AI-driven data centers is driving demand for intelligent and high-density power distribution solutions. Modern PDUs are increasingly integrated with digital infrastructure management platforms, enabling capabilities such as real-time monitoring, remote management, outlet-level control, and energy optimization, supporting efficient and reliable operation across enterprise, colocation, and cloud data center environments.

Schneider Electric (France), Vertiv (US), Eaton (Ireland), Legrand (France), and ABB (Switzerland) are among the major players in the data center PDU market. ## Abstract

We empirically investigate differences in cost-effectiveness of public procurement auctions that are won by entrants and incumbents. We use a unique data set that covers all Turkish public procurement auctions for the years 2004 to 2010. We find that procurement prices of auctions won by entrants are significantly lower. More than half of the entrants cannot survive in the public procurement market and cannot win more than one auction. Our results indicate that policymakers should cultivate competition and promote entry in public procurement auctions.

# Bid Average Methods in Procurement

## Abstract

We empirically investigate incumbents' and entrants' bids on an original dataset of 192 scoring rule auctions for canteen services in Italy. Our findings show that winning rebates are lower (i.e., prices paid by the public buyer are higher) when the contract is awarded to the incumbent supplier. This result is not explained by the observable characteristics of the auction or the service awarded. We develop a simple theoretical model showing that the result is consistent with a setting in which the buyer exploits specific information on the incumbent supplier's production cost.
